chunks.text: Eli Yablonovitch is Professor Emeritus and Professor in the Graduate School at UC Berkeley. His contact information includes office location at 267M Cory Hall, phone 642-6821, and email eliy@eecs.berkeley.edu. Office hours require emailing in advance. He has links to his personal homepage, publications, dissertations, and research support through Stephanie Bratton (sbratton@berkeley.edu).

Eli Yablonovitch introduced strained semiconductor lasers for better performance due to reduced hole effective mass, which are used in optical telecommunication. He is considered a Father of the Photonic BandGap concept and coined the term 'Photonic Crystal'. The first experimental Photonic bandgap structure is called 'Yablonovite'. In photovoltaics, he introduced the 4(n squared) 'Yablonovitch Limit' light-trapping factor used in commercial solar panels.

Yablonovitch's mantra that 'a great solar cell also needs to be a great LED' led to world record solar cells: 29.1% single-junction, 31.5% dual-junction, and 38.8% quadruple-junction efficiency at 1 sun. His startup Ethertronics shipped over 2 billion cellphone antennas. He co-founded Luxtera Inc., a Silicon Photonics leader acquired by Cisco, with 2D Photonic Crystals in chips used globally in data centers.

Prof. Yablonovitch is elected to the National Academy of Engineering, National Academy of Sciences, National Academy of Inventors, American Academy of Arts & Sciences, and is a Foreign Member of the Royal Society of London. His awards include the OSA Ives-Quinn Medal, Benjamin Franklin Medal, IEEE Edison Medal, Isaac Newton Medal, Buckley Prize, IEEE Cherry solar cell award, Rank Prize, Harvey Prize, IEEE Photonics Award, Mountbatten Medal, Julius Springer Prize, R.W. Wood Prize, Streifer Award, and Adolf Lomb Medal. He holds honorary Ph.D.s from KTH Stockholm, HKUST, McGill, and is honorary Professor at Nanjing University.

Eli Yablonovitch is Director Emeritus of the NSF Center for Energy Efficient Electronics Science (E3S) at Berkeley. He earned his Ph.D. in Applied Physics from Harvard in 1972. His career includes work at Bell Telephone Labs, professor at Harvard, research at Exxon on solar energy, Distinguished Member at Bell Communications Research, Northrop-Grumman Chair at UCLA, and James & Katherine Lau Chair Emeritus at UC Berkeley from 2007.

Yablonovitch's education includes a 1972 Ph.D. and 1969 A.M. in Applied Physics from Harvard University, and a 1967 B.Sc. in Physics from McGill University. His research areas are Physical Electronics (PHY), Physics-Based Computing for Optimization & Deep Learning; Photonic Crystals at optical and microwave frequencies; Optical Antennas; and Solar Cells. He is involved with the Center for Energy Efficient Electronics Science (E3S).

Selected publications include work on van der Waals heterostructures (2014), quantum optical absorption in 2D semiconductors (2013), luminescence in solar cells approaching the Shockley–Queisser limit (2012), circuit analysis in metal-optics (2012), and an article on Photonic Crystals as semiconductors of light (2001). These highlight his contributions to materials science, photonics, and photovoltaics.

Selected honors and awards include the Frederic Ives Medal/Quinn Prize (2019), Benjamin Franklin Medal (2018), IEEE Edison Medal (2018), IEEE Cherry Award (2017), NAI Fellow (2017), Buckley Prize (2016), Isaac Newton Medal (2015), Rank Prize (2014), Royal Society Foreign Member (2013), IEEE Photonics Award (2012), and earlier recognitions like NAS Member (2003) and Adolf Lomb Medal (1978).
